Let's take a look at activating unity through perceptions of oneness. Most people would agree that they're more likely to grant requests from members of their family or to volunteer to help those who are kin. Anyone can be important to us, but ties of blood, marriage, and adoption tend to create the strongest feelings of oneness. This was shown in a study by Chinese researchers who asked people to think about their relationships with relatives of varying levels of genetic closeness to them. Parents, for example, were compared to siblings, who were in turn compared to distant cousins. The researchers asked the participants to think about how often they had social interactions with each of these relatives. Finally, the researchers asked each participant to position two on-screen circles, one representing themselves and the other representing the relative in question, so that they illustrated the degree of self-other overlap that is unity that they felt with the other person. The researchers indeed found that the closer the relatives were genetically, the more self-other overlap the participants felt with them. Well, this finding makes sense. Hardly surprising. But here's the question. How can we apply the lesson to our business relationships with people who aren't our kin? The researchers also found that kinship closeness wasn't the only factor that increased a sense of unity inside the relationships. Stronger feelings of unity also characterized the relationships in which the participants had interacted frequently with those they were rating. We can elevate feelings of we-ness in a relationship by raising the amount of contact we have with our relationship partner, providing it is a negative contact, of course. Think about your work team and business contacts. Use the pairs of circles that the Chinese scientists used to illustrate the overlap between you and the individuals you'd like to influence more on the job, such as a valuable colleague or your manager or one of your better customers or clients or a new business relationship you would like to have or even a damaged business relationship partner. Once you're finished dragging the circles to match how much unity you feel, ask yourself, are there opportunities to increase the frequency of interaction with these people? Because that's been shown to increase feelings of we-ness inside the relationship.
